name: Az-Zahir
title: Az-Zahir: The Manifest
meta_description: Az-Zahir, the Manifest, built from the Qur'an and classical tafsir. The name above which there is nothing and the One whose signs cover everything you can see, paired with Al-Batin, nearer than nothing else. The verse the Prophet ﷺ called better than a thousand, and the words to say when doubt stirs in your chest. Qur'an verified.
keywords: Az-Zahir, Az-Zaahir, The Manifest, Al-Batin, names of Allah, 99 names, Surah Al-Hadid, hu al-awwal wal-akhir, nothing above You, dua before sleep, signs of Allah, Asma ul Husna, Buruja
source_note: Grounded in the Qur'an (Sahih International, verified via quran.ai) and classical tafsir (Ibn Kathir, Tafsir as-Sa'di, and al-Tafsir al-Muyassar), in the voice of Buruja. The famous supplication of the Prophet ﷺ that names Allah Az-Zahir is a hadith recorded by Muslim, cited here as hadith, not as Qur'an.
lede: Some of the names of Allah meet a fear in you, and some meet a kind of forgetting. This one meets the forgetting. We move through a world so full of Allah's signs that we stop seeing them, the way you stop hearing a sound that never stops. The sky, the faces, the food on the table, the breath going in and out without your permission, all of it pointing somewhere, and most days we look straight through it. This name turns your eyes back on.

Az-Zahir, the Manifest. He is the One whose existence is the most obvious thing there is, plainer than the sunlight you read this by, because everything you can see is a sign of Him and there is nothing higher than Him to be seen. And the Qur'an almost never lets this name stand alone. It sets it beside Al-Batin, the Intimate, the One nearer to you than anything, so that in a single breath you learn that the same Allah is both utterly above you and utterly close, manifest over all and hidden within reach.

sections.h2: The name, and the name that walks beside it

ayah.arabic: هُوَ الْأَوَّلُ وَالْآخِرُ وَالظَّاهِرُ وَالْبَاطِنُ ۖ وَهُوَ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ عَلِيمٌ
ayah.text: He is the First and the Last, the Ascendant and the Intimate, and He is, of all things, Knowing.

paras: Start with the name itself. Az-Zahir comes from three Arabic letters, za, ha, ra, the root of zuhur, which carries the sense of something that becomes apparent, that comes out into the open, that rises into view. From the same root the Qur'an speaks of what is zahir, the outward and visible, set against what is batin, the inward and hidden. So at its plainest the name says: Allah is the Manifest, the One made evident, whose reality is the most clearly shown thing in all of existence.

Notice where the name sits. It does not arrive on its own. Here near the opening of Surah Al-Hadid it is the third of four names poured out in one breath: He is the First and the Last, the Manifest and the Intimate. Al-Awwal and Al-Akhir bracket all of time, the One before the first thing and the One after the last. And then Az-Zahir and Al-Batin bracket all of space and nearness, the One above whom there is nothing and the One nearer than whom there is nothing. Between the four names there is no edge of your existence, no before, no after, no above, no within, that is not already filled by Him.

Commenting on this verse, al-Sa'di gives the four names their tightest gloss. Az-Zahir, he writes, is the One above whom there is nothing, and Al-Batin is the One nearer than whom there is nothing, and then he seals the verse by saying that Allah's knowledge encompasses the outward and the inward, the secrets and the hidden things, the matters that came before and the ones still to come. So the manifest and the hidden are not two halves of God. They are two ways of saying that nothing, high or low, seen or unseen, is outside Him.

There is a precise meaning packed into Az-Zahir that the mufassirun keep returning to, and it is worth slowing down for. To call Allah the Manifest is, in one sense, to say that He is the Highest, the One over all things, with nothing above Him at all.

The clearest place this is spelled out is not in a definition but in a prayer the Prophet ﷺ used to make at night, which Ibn Kathir records under this very verse and which is preserved in the collection of Muslim. In it the Prophet ﷺ calls on Allah by all four of these names and explains each one as he goes. Of this name he says: and You are Az-Zahir, so there is nothing above You. That is the gloss straight from the Sunnah. Manifest here means uppermost, supreme, the One over everything, so that when you lift your gaze as high as it will go, past the sky, past whatever is past the sky, there is nothing standing over Him. He is above it all.

Sit with what that does to the size of your troubles. Every power you are afraid of, every authority that seems to tower over your small life, the people who decide things about you, the systems that feel immovable, the fears that loom, all of them have something above them, and that something is Allah, who has nothing above Him. To know Az-Zahir is to know that the highest thing in existence is not any of the things that frighten you. It is the One you can turn to directly, with nothing and no one ranked over His head.

sections.h2: The most obvious thing there is

ayah.arabic: أَلَمْ تَرَوْا أَنَّ اللَّهَ سَخَّرَ لَكُم مَّا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَمَا فِي الْأَرْضِ وَأَسْبَغَ عَلَيْكُمْ نِعَمَهُ ظَاهِرَةً وَبَاطِنَةً
ayah.text: Do you not see that Allah has made subject to you whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth and amply bestowed upon you His favors, [both] apparent and unapparent?

paras: There is a second sense inside Az-Zahir, and it is the one that reaches your daily life most directly. To say Allah is the Manifest is also to say that He is the most evident reality there is, because everything your eyes land on is a sign pointing back to Him. He is hidden from sight in His essence, and at the same time more obvious than anything, because nothing exists that is not His evidence.

Listen to how the Qur'an presses this in Surah Luqman. Do you not see, it asks, almost impatiently, that Allah made everything in the heavens and the earth serve you, and poured His favours over you, both the zahira and the batina, the apparent and the hidden? The same root as the name. Commenting on this, al-Sa'di reads asbagha, poured, as meaning He overwhelmed you and covered you in His blessings, the apparent ones we can see and the hidden ones we cannot, the favours of this world and the favours of the religion, the bringing of every benefit and the warding off of every harm. The signs are not rare or far away. They are draped over you, head to foot.

We might reflect that this is the real reason Az-Zahir meets our forgetting rather than our fear. The problem was never that the evidence was thin. The problem is that it is everywhere, and a thing that is everywhere becomes invisible. The fish, it is said, is the last to discover water. The Manifest is the air your whole life is suspended in, and this name is the moment you notice the air.

sections.h2: The verse worth more than a thousand
paras: If you want to feel the weight the early Muslims gave this verse, look at how the Prophet ﷺ treated the surah it sits in. Ibn Kathir opens his commentary on Surah Al-Hadid with a report, carried from al-Irbad ibn Sariya, that the Prophet ﷺ used to recite the Musabbihat, the surahs that open by declaring Allah's glory, before he slept, and that he said of them: in them there is an ayah better than a thousand ayat. And Ibn Kathir notes that the ayah being pointed to is, and Allah knows best, this one: He is the First and the Last, the Manifest and the Intimate, and He is, of all things, Knowing.

Why would these four names be weighed so heavily? Because of what they do to a heart that is shaking. Ibn Kathir follows the report with a striking exchange. A man came to Ibn Abbas and admitted he sometimes felt something unsettling stir in his chest, some whisper of doubt. Ibn Abbas laughed, and said no one is free of that, and then told him: when you find that feeling in yourself, recite, He is the First and the Last, the Manifest and the Intimate, and He is, of all things, Knowing. The cure he reached for was not an argument. It was this verse.

Think about why it works. Doubt makes God feel distant, abstract, maybe absent. And these four names answer that not with a proof but with a sheer enclosure: He is before everything, after everything, above everything, within everything, and He knows all of it. There is no gap left for the doubt to live in. To say Az-Zahir when your faith trembles is to remind yourself that the One you are unsure of is more present than the very thought doubting Him.

sections.h2: Manifest, and hidden in the same breath

ayah.arabic: وَهُوَ مَعَكُمْ أَيْنَ مَا كُنتُمْ ۚ وَاللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ
ayah.text: And He is with you wherever you are. And Allah, of what you do, is Seeing.

paras: Here is the part that can unsettle you at first and then becomes the sweetest thing in the name. Az-Zahir, the Manifest, the One above all, is paired with Al-Batin, the Intimate, the One closer than close. The two are said together, and they are not in tension. They are the whole truth.

Watch how the surah itself moves. One verse names Him the Manifest and the Intimate. The very next verse, which al-Sa'di and the other mufassirun read as flowing straight on from it, says: and He is with you wherever you are. The God with nothing above Him is, in the same passage, the God who is with you in the room you are sitting in. The same prayer of the Prophet ﷺ recorded by Muslim makes the pairing explicit: You are Az-Zahir, so there is nothing above You, and You are Al-Batin, so there is nothing nearer than You. Height and nearness, both at once, both total.

This is the answer to a quiet ache a lot of believers carry. We imagine we have to choose between a God who is majestic, exalted, far above us, and a God who is close enough to whisper to. The names refuse the choice. He is the most high and the most near in one breath. al-Muyassar, glossing the same verse, keeps both edges in view: the Manifest above whom there is nothing, the Intimate nearer than whom there is nothing, and nothing hidden from Him in the earth or the sky. You never have to shout up to a distant throne. The One on the throne, with nothing above Him, is already nearer to you than your own pulse.

sections.h2: Live as someone who can see Him everywhere
paras: A name of Allah is never only information. It is meant to change how you move through a day, and Az-Zahir changes you in at least three ways.

First, it reopens your eyes. The whole burden of this name is that the evidence of Allah is poured over you, apparent and hidden, as al-Sa'di said of the favours in Surah Luqman. So the work is not to find new proof. It is to stop sleeping through the proof you are swimming in. Look at the next sunrise as a sign and not a backdrop. Treat the food, the health, the people who love you, the breath you did not have to ask for, as the manifest favours they are. A heart shaped by Az-Zahir walks through an ordinary street and sees it crowded with God.

Second, it shrinks what frightens you. If Allah is the One above whom there is nothing, then nothing you fear is actually at the top. Every power over your life has a power over it, and that power answers to no one. So you can stand in front of the intimidating thing, the boss, the diagnosis, the debt, the future, and quietly know it is not the highest thing in the room. The Manifest is. Take your need straight to Him, over the heads of everything that looms.

Third, it steadies you when faith wobbles. Ibn Abbas handed a doubting man this exact verse as a cure. So when the whisper comes, that maybe no one is there, that maybe it is all empty, you do not have to win a debate in your head. You reach, as he was told to reach, for the names: the First, the Last, the Manifest, the Intimate. The God you are doubting is before you, after you, above you, and within you, and He knows the doubt itself. That is a fortress doubt cannot find a wall to climb.

sections.h2: The One you were looking at all along
paras: Step back and let the size of it land. You have spent your whole life inside the evidence. Every face you have ever loved, every sky you have ever stood under, every meal, every recovery from sickness, every small mercy you forgot to thank Him for, all of it has been Az-Zahir making Himself known to you, quietly, relentlessly, since before you could speak. The Manifest was never hard to find. He was the thing too close and too constant to notice.

And He was never only far. The name that tells you nothing stands above Him is fastened in the Qur'an to the name that tells you nothing comes between you and Him. So the most exalted reality in existence is also the most intimate one in your life. You do not climb to Him. You wake up to Him, already here, already nearer than the words you are reading, already above everything that ever made you afraid.

That is the gift hidden inside this name. The forgetting underneath so many of our days, that we move through a world emptied of God, is answered not with a lecture but with a name of God. He is Az-Zahir. He has been in plain sight the whole time, and the moment you turn to look, you find He was never anywhere but here.

dua.arabic: اللَّهُمَّ أَنتَ الْأَوَّلُ فَلَيْسَ قَبْلَكَ شَيْءٌ، وَأَنتَ الْآخِرُ فَلَيْسَ بَعْدَكَ شَيْءٌ، وَأَنتَ الظَّاهِرُ فَلَيْسَ فَوْقَكَ شَيْءٌ، وَأَنتَ الْبَاطِنُ فَلَيْسَ دُونَكَ شَيْءٌ
dua.translit: Allahumma anta al-Awwalu fa laysa qablaka shay, wa anta al-Akhiru fa laysa badaka shay, wa anta az-Zahiru fa laysa fawqaka shay, wa anta al-Batinu fa laysa dunaka shay
dua.en: O Allah, You are the First, so there is nothing before You; You are the Last, so there is nothing after You; You are the Manifest, so there is nothing above You; You are the Intimate, so there is nothing nearer than You. (Hadith, recorded by Muslim.)

lessons.text: The evidence of Allah is poured over you, apparent and hidden, as al-Sa'di says of the favours in Surah Luqman. Look at the sunrise, the food, the breath you did not ask for, as signs and not scenery. The Manifest is not hard to find, only easy to walk past.

Az-Zahir means the One above whom there is nothing, as the Prophet ﷺ explained in the hadith Muslim records. Every power that towers over you has a power over it, and that power answers to no one. Take your need straight to the highest thing in the room.

You do not have to choose between a God too exalted to reach and a God close enough to whisper to. Az-Zahir is paired with Al-Batin, the One nearer than whom there is nothing, while 57:4 says He is with you wherever you are. He is most high and most near in one breath.

lessons.bold: Reach for the verse when doubt stirs.
lessons.text: Ibn Kathir relates that Ibn Abbas told a man troubled by doubt to recite this verse: the First, the Last, the Manifest, the Intimate. When the whisper comes, do not argue it down. Name the One who is before, after, above, and within you, and who knows the doubt itself.

lessons.bold: Let the unseen weigh as much as the seen.
lessons.text: His favours come apparent and hidden, and He knows the secret as plainly as the spoken. Tend the inward life, the intentions, the private prayer, the quiet gratitude, knowing the Manifest who sees your acts is also the Intimate who reads what is folded in your chest.

faq.q: What does the name Az-Zahir mean?
faq.a: Az-Zahir (الظاهر) means The Manifest, from the root z-h-r, the sense of what is apparent, evident, or risen into view. Commenting on Surah Al-Hadid 57:3, al-Sa'di explains it as the One above whom there is nothing, and the Prophet ﷺ, in a supplication recorded by Muslim, glossed it the same way: You are Az-Zahir, so there is nothing above You. It carries two linked senses: that Allah is the Highest, supreme over all, and that He is the most evident reality there is, because everything you can see is a sign pointing to Him.

faq.q: Why is Az-Zahir paired with Al-Batin?
faq.a: The Qur'an joins them in a single breath in 57:3: the Manifest and the Intimate. al-Sa'di reads Az-Zahir as the One above whom there is nothing and Al-Batin as the One nearer than whom there is nothing, so between them they close off both height and nearness. The pairing answers a common worry, that we must choose between a God who is exalted and far and a God who is close. The names say He is both at once: the most high and the most near. The very next verse adds, and He is with you wherever you are (57:4).

faq.q: Is the famous supplication that names Allah Az-Zahir from the Qur'an?
faq.a: No. The verse that names Allah Az-Zahir is Qur'an (Al-Hadid 57:3). But the well known supplication, 'O Allah, You are the First, so there is nothing before You... You are Az-Zahir, so there is nothing above You, and You are Al-Batin, so there is nothing nearer than You, settle our debt for us and free us from poverty,' is a hadith, the words of the Prophet ﷺ at bedtime, recorded by Muslim and by Ahmad from Abu Hurayra. Ibn Kathir cites it under this verse. It is a Prophetic prayer, not a Quranic verse.

faq.q: Why did Ibn Abbas recommend reciting this verse against doubt?
faq.a: Ibn Kathir relates that a man told Ibn Abbas he felt unsettling whispers in his chest, and Ibn Abbas, after noting no one is entirely free of that, told him to recite 57:3 whenever the feeling came: the First, the Last, the Manifest, the Intimate, and He is of all things Knowing. Doubt works by making God feel distant or absent, and these four names answer it not with an argument but by leaving no gap at all: Allah is before, after, above, and within everything, and He knows it all. There is nowhere for the doubt to settle.

closing_heading: Why this name stays with us
closing: We carry a quiet forgetting, that we move through a world somehow emptied of God, looking straight through a million signs without seeing the One they point to. Az-Zahir is the answer the Qur'an gives to that forgetting, not as an idea but as a name of God. He is the Manifest, the One above whom there is nothing, whose evidence is poured over us apparent and hidden, more obvious than the light we read by and too constant to notice. And the Qur'an fastens that height to nearness, pairing Him with Al-Batin and adding that He is with us wherever we are, so the most exalted reality in existence is also the most intimate one in our lives. To know this name is to wake up, mid life, and find that the One we were looking for was the One we were looking at all along.

O Allah, Az-Zahir, the Manifest, You are above all things and nearer than all things, and Your signs cover everything our eyes can reach. Open our eyes to the evidence we sleep through, put every fear beneath You where it belongs, steady our hearts when doubt stirs, and let us live awake to a world full of You. You are the First, so there is nothing before You; You are the Last, so there is nothing after You; You are the Manifest, so there is nothing above You; You are the Intimate, so there is nothing nearer than You.

slides:
Az-Zahir.

The Manifest.

We move through a world so full of Allah's signs that we have stopped seeing them, the way you stop hearing a sound that never stops.

This name turns your eyes back on.

It sits in a verse with three other names:

'He is the First and the Last, the Manifest and the Intimate, and He is, of all things, Knowing.' (57:3)

Before, after, above, within. No edge of your existence is outside Him.

What does Az-Zahir mean?

In the hadith Muslim records, the Prophet ﷺ explained it himself:

**You are Az-Zahir, so there is nothing above You.**

The Manifest is the Highest. The One over everything.

Sit with that.

Every power you fear has something above it. Every authority that towers over your small life answers to something higher.

And the highest thing of all is the One you can turn to directly, with no one ranked over His head.

There is a second sense.

Az-Zahir is also the most **obvious** reality there is, because everything you see is a sign of Him.

'He poured His favours over you, apparent and hidden.' (31:20)

The signs are not far away. They are draped over you, head to foot.

The fish is the last to discover water.

The Manifest is the air your whole life is suspended in. A thing that is everywhere becomes invisible.

This name is the moment you notice the air.

The Prophet ﷺ called this verse one worth more than a thousand.

And when a man came to Ibn Abbas shaken by doubt, his cure was not an argument.

It was this: recite 'He is the First and the Last, the Manifest and the Intimate.'

Here is the sweetest part.

The One above whom there is nothing is paired with Al-Batin, the One **nearer** than whom there is nothing.

'And He is with you wherever you are.' (57:4)

Most high and most near, in one breath. 🤍

He was never hard to find.

He was the thing too close and too constant to notice.

Turn to look, and you find Az-Zahir was never anywhere but here.

Ya Zahir.
social.caption:
We carry a quiet forgetting: that we move through a world somehow emptied of God, looking straight through a million signs without seeing the One they point to.

Az-Zahir, the Manifest, is the answer the Qur'an gives. Grounded in classical tafsir (Ibn Kathir, al-Sa'di, al-Muyassar), this name carries two linked senses. It means the Highest, the One above whom there is nothing, as the Prophet ﷺ explained in the supplication Muslim records: 'You are Az-Zahir, so there is nothing above You.' And it means the most obvious reality there is, because everything you can see is His sign. As Allah says, He poured His favours over you, apparent and hidden (31:20). The evidence was never thin. It is everywhere, and a thing that is everywhere becomes invisible.

The Prophet ﷺ called the verse this name sits in, 57:3, better than a thousand verses. And when a man came to Ibn Abbas shaken by doubt, the cure he was given was simply to recite it: He is the First and the Last, the Manifest and the Intimate, and He is, of all things, Knowing. There is no gap left for doubt to live in.

And the most exalted name is fastened to the most intimate one: Az-Zahir, above all, is paired with Al-Batin, nearer than all, while the next verse adds, He is with you wherever you are (57:4).

Which sign will you stop and actually see today? He has been in plain view the whole time. 🤍

Note: the bedtime supplication quoted here is a hadith recorded by Muslim, not a verse of the Qur'an.

email.subject: The One you were looking at all along
email.alt_subject: The name above which there is nothing
email.preview: Az-Zahir, the Manifest. The most exalted reality in existence, and the most easily overlooked one in your day.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

Some of the names of Allah meet a fear in you. This one meets a forgetting. We move through a world so full of Allah's signs that we have stopped seeing them, the way you stop hearing a sound that never stops: the sky, the faces, the food, the breath going in and out without your permission, all of it pointing somewhere, and most days we look straight through it.

One of the names of Allah turns your eyes back on: Az-Zahir, the Manifest.

It sits in a single verse alongside three other names. He is the First and the Last, the Manifest and the Intimate, and He is, of all things, Knowing (Al-Hadid 57:3). Al-Awwal and Al-Akhir bracket all of time. Az-Zahir and Al-Batin bracket all of height and nearness: the One above whom there is nothing, and the One nearer than whom there is nothing.

What does it mean to call Allah the Manifest? The Prophet ﷺ explained it himself, in a supplication recorded by Muslim: You are Az-Zahir, so there is nothing above You. The Manifest is the Highest, the One over everything. Sit with what that does to the size of your troubles. Every power you are afraid of has something above it, and that something is Allah, who has nothing above Him. The highest thing in existence is not any of the things that frighten you. It is the One you can turn to directly.

There is a second sense, and it reaches your daily life most. Az-Zahir is also the most obvious reality there is, because everything your eyes land on is a sign of Him. The Qur'an presses this in Surah Luqman: do you not see that Allah poured His favours over you, both apparent and hidden? (31:20). Commenting on it, al-Sa'di says Allah overwhelmed you and covered you in blessings, the ones you can see and the ones you cannot. The signs are not rare. They are draped over you, head to foot. The trouble was never that the evidence was thin. It is that a thing that is everywhere becomes invisible.

The Prophet ﷺ called the verse this name sits in better than a thousand verses. And Ibn Kathir relates that when a man came to Ibn Abbas troubled by whispers of doubt, the cure he was handed was not an argument, but this: recite, He is the First and the Last, the Manifest and the Intimate. Doubt makes God feel distant. These names leave no gap for it: He is before, after, above, and within everything, and He knows it all.

And here is the sweetest part. The One above whom there is nothing is paired with Al-Batin, the One nearer than whom there is nothing, and the very next verse adds, and He is with you wherever you are (57:4). You do not climb to Him. You wake up to Him, already here, already nearer than these words, already above everything that ever made you afraid.

Ya Zahir, open our eyes to the signs we sleep through, and keep us awake to a world full of You.

If you would like the full reflection, we have written it here:
